section_title: INTRODUCTION TO MASTERING HIGH PROBABILITY SCALPING VOL. 2
content:
Welcome back to Volume Two of Three for Mastering High Probability Scalping. This volume focuses on
previous day bank liquidity runs. We will be reviewing the daily bias to ensure everyone understands the
process, specifically referring to a daily chart.

- SWING HIGH BREAK
When implementing the daily bias on a daily chart, for a bullish scenario, we are waiting for a swing high to
form and to be broken. A swing high is defined as a candle that has a lower high to the left of it and then a
lower high to the right of it. If at any time in the future it is traded through, we have a bullish stage. This
means we:
- Anticipate a future buying opportunity.
- Are now on an alert to wait for a specific criteria.

- SWING LOW FORMATION AND OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY
Following a broken swing high, the next criterion is to look for a swing low to form. A swing low is defined as
a candle that has a higher low to the left of it and a higher low to the right of it, again on the daily chart. A
key point is that this swing low should not take out a previous recent swing low. If we have this criteria
immediately after a high is broken, we have the probable optimal trade entry scenario by itself on a daily
chart. This does not mean it has to line up with a 62 percent retracing level. It just means that we are in
effect trading with a higher probability after breaking a short-term high, followed by a retracement, so
therefore the market should have an ability to find momentum on the upside.

- ANTICIPATING THE THIRD CANDLE RAID
When the swing low forms, we anticipate the high of its third candle to be rated or traded through the very
next day. Preferably, the next trading day where it opens, you want to see it open below the third candle of
the swing low's high. We look for previous day's highs to be rated each day until a daily swing high forms or
price reaches a key support or resistance level.

section_title: SUMMARY OF BULLISH SCENARIO CRITERIA AND MAINTENANCE
content:
The stage for a bullish scenario is:
- Anytime a swing high is broken and traded to a new high.
- Expect a
retracement.
- Wait for a daily swing low to form.
- The swing low should be a higher swing low than any
recent previous short-term swing low.
- The high of the third candle that makes the swing low: you want to
see the next candle open below that candle's high.
- Anticipate the market to run through this candle's high.
- Look for previous day's highs to be rated each day until a daily swing high forms or price reaches a key
support-resistance level. There will be a lot of missed opportunities admittedly with this, but it gives you a
specific criteria on which to work.

- SWING LOW BREAK
When employing the daily bias for a bearish scenario, we are waiting again on the daily chart for a swing
low to be broken. A swing low is defined as a daily candle that has a higher low to the left of it and a higher
low to the right of it. Eventually, as price trades through this, this break in market structure sets the stage
for a bearish market condition. So we are alerted to waiting for a sell scenario.

- SWING HIGH FORMATION AND ANTICIPATION
We wait for a retracement when we get the daily swing high formed. The third candle that makes the swing
high, we are going to be anticipating that low to be violated the very next trading day. A key point is we do
not want to see this swing high break a previous swing high immediately before the swing low that has
broken a previous swing low. So what we are doing is we are looking for a swing low broken here, so now
we have the ability to see the market trade to a new low, then it retraces but will not break a swing high. So
now we are getting a net three-quarter back retracement. By itself, it is like an optimal trade entry; it does
not have to be 62 to 70.5 to 79 percent, it is better if it does, but it does not require it.

- DAILY SWING HIGH AND LOW RAID SUMMARY
You want to see the market trade up until it creates a daily swing high. When that third candle forms at its
close, the very next trading day you will be watching for price to make an attempt to trade through this
candle's low. That remains the bias each trading day until a swing low forms or we trade down to an old low
or another significant low. Again, in summary:
- Look for a swing low to form on the daily chart, and then for
it to be broken.
- Look for a swing high to form, but it does not break a recent swing high.
- When the swing
high forms, anticipate the third daily candle in the swing high.
- Look for its low to be rated or traded
through the following day.
- Look for the previous day's low to be rated each day until a new daily swing low
on a daily forms or price reaches a key support-resistance level.

You are not going to have a setup every single trading day. I have gotten a lot of emails so far since I have
produced the first volume of this three-part series, and the common complaint I am getting is 'I am not
getting a setup every day,' and it has not been promised. If you look at every major that is paired against
the dollar, you can get about three to four solid setups per week. This means you are probably not going to
get a setup every single trading day. Chances are, one pair among all the ones that are available will
provide you a setup to study so you can practice in your demo account with it. The emphasis is for you to
remember that you only need about 25 pips or so per week. If you risk 2 percent of your account (which
may be high admittedly for some of you, but if you have grown in your understanding about what I am
teaching and you are willing to risk 2 percent), it takes a little bit less than 25 pips per week to make 6
percent compounded monthly. I think that is the objective that folks should be looking for when you are
new, because it is realistic, it is low, but yet it still doubles the account over the course of a twelve-month
year.

If we are looking for one good setup that would yield that 25 pips or so, you only need one scalp, one setup
that does that. I started the current teaching week on Twitter, kind of building the idea of making 50 pips per
week. If you frame your scalps in such a way that it allows you to aim for 25 pips, you only really need two
setups for a week, don't you? I like that model personally because it is very close to what I do as a short
- term or intraday trader. I know the likelihood is I want to be trading on Tuesdays and Wednesdays. If I can
get my entire weekly objective, which is 50 to 75 pips per week, if I can get that done in one day, then I will
not do any more trading after that, regardless of what day of the week it is. But usually I hone in on
Tuesday and Wednesday because they are primarily the best days.

section_title: SYNCHRONIZING WITH WEEKLY OUTLOOK AND MARKET CONVICTION
content:
Whether bullish or bearish, if it is bullish for the week, then I am looking for the weekly low to form around
Tuesday or Wednesday's New York open. If I can anticipate a lower close or weekly bearish candle, I am
shooting with an expectation that we are going to be seeing lower prices by Friday's close relative to
Sunday's opening, then I will be looking for Tuesday or Wednesday's price action to create the high of the
week. So if I can trade in sync with that idea, it also formulates a lot more conviction and confidence behind
the setups that I am looking to trade. Especially with what is being described here, it is a rather simplistic
approach. It may have been an oversimplification on my part by way of creating this diagram, but from an
internal standpoint, how I view the marketplace, this is what I am looking for. If I see it on the daily chart,
chances are the daily chart will probably sustain the move for a few days, and you will only need one
trading day. So if you are having a scenario that is bullish or bearish relative to what I just described here
so far in this video, chances are you are probably going to have one day's worth of momentum, and that is
all you need. Every single pair does not move lockstep to one another; they are not always moving in
tandem. So what may be a good pair to trade today may not be a pair that is really good to trade tomorrow,
but another pair may move in equal or better fashion. In other words, the setups are plenty, but you have to
allow them to be presented in price action from the daily chart and then not forcing it.

Let's take a look over at the charts; I will give you an example of what it looks like and we can use the Kill
Zones. We are looking at the cable (GBP/USD), this is a daily chart. Let's stroll through just some random
place, it does not make a difference where we start out. I want you to take a look at the price action here
and we are going to look at this swing low right here, and it is on the heels of a previous short-term high.
So we have a short-term high here: lower high to the left, lower high to the right, highest high in the middle.
It breaks that, so now we are on a buy watch for scalping. This is a swing high that is broken, and we have
to wait for a swing low to form after this is broken. So we are going to be anticipating a retracement after
this run-up. As price starts to drop down, we have a preliminary swing low here. This candle is the third
one; we need to make sure we wait for price to trade through this candle's high. The very next day it does
not do that. Now we have another candle form a potential swing low here. So this candle here, we have to
wait for this candle's high to be traded through. The very next candle, it does not do it, it does it here. So
now we can be a buyer here with a scalp running previous day's high which is this one. So on July 24th,
2017, that high again coming in at 1.3057, that is where the liquidity run is going to be right there. This
candle on the 25th of July is where we will be looking for the setup right there.

We are going to drop down into a smaller timeframe; we will use a 15-minute timeframe. I will scrunch this
up, the day dividers are in, so you guys can see it. Previous day's high is right here. I will put a horizontal
line on the chart to facilitate that. So here is where the liquidity is, we are running for 1.3057. On this day
here, we are looking for a scenario to get long to run that liquidity pool. When I use the market sessions, I
am looking for London setups and New York setups primarily. This is a London session low, and I will let
you see that here, that is in London. We are going to use that as I mentioned in Volume One, I use session
highs and lows. This is the highest portion of the day in terms of a 15-minute candle. You just put it right
here, you guys can see it. So I am using this one and this one framing the entire price move. So as price
starts this day here, I am not concerned about anything until it gets down into the optimal trade entry 62
percent retracing level, respectively 70.5 and 79 percent.

You can see price trades it, it hits it here. This candle comes in exactly 9:45, that is London, and we are
going into a nice run into previous day's high. So you can see that was a nice little scalp. It offered as much
as 10, 20, 30, 40 pips to get to the high. If we look for 10 to 20 pips of a sweep above this high, it takes us
10, 20 here, so 20 pips, 40 pips, 60 pips, 70 pips or so in terms of potential price range. That in itself is it for
the week for me. That would be it. Why would you do anything else? It is hard to believe, but that is how I
operate. I do not look for a whole lot of setups per week because I am content with doing one thing well,
and there it is. Now, if this were a more conservative approach, you could be looking for the long down here
based on the optimal trade entry and reaching just to the old high. Now if that is the case, say you are filled
at, we will just round it to 1.3020, that again is getting out at 1.3057. It is 37 pips or so, almost 40 pips. Let
us say 40 pips. You only need to do one more trade for the week for 10 pips to get a 50-pip net return for
the week.

section_title: DEFINITIVE TARGETS VS. UNLIMITED PROFIT MINDSET
content:
Now there are a lot of folks that will say, 'Do not set targets, do not set goals for daily or weekly because
you do not know what the market is going to do.' Well, I would submit that that is partially true; we do not
know with any assurity what the market is going to do, but we do have pretty strong probabilities of
wherever the market may reach for. If we can frame the idea of where we are trying to get in at based on
time and price, Kill Zone, and such an optimal trade entry price, and where we are reaching for, does that
not offer us a definitive way of determining what could be reasonably expected for that particular setup? If
we know that we are looking at an intraday setup like this, there are typically five days per week, unless it is
a holiday or the markets are not trading because of some other bank holiday or whatever. We can see
many instances where 25 to 50 pips is rather easy to get. It is when we get into the 'I want to make 250 to
500 pips per week' then it becomes a little daunting in terms of a task. So I think by focusing on 20-25 pips
per setup and aiming for two good ones per week, I think 50 pips is a really low-hanging fruit, reachable,
achievable, and certainly realistic in the scope of a developing trader. Will you get it every week? No. Will
you get it right out the gate starting using my concepts? No. But over time you will eventually grow into that
and an expectancy for a nice return of 50 pips per week.
